ORDER ADOPTING THE TENTATIVE AND DENYING PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO ENFORCE SETTLEMENT PURSUANT TO CCP 664.6

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF ALLAN QUITON'S MOTION TO ENFORCE SETTLEMENT PER CCP 664.6. THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENTATIVE RULING. DENIED. THE SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT OF JANUARY 14, 2019 DOES NOT CONTAIN ANY LANGUAGE BINDING THE PARTIES TO ENTER INTO A MUTUAL RELEASE OF CLAIMS. THE WAIVER OF CIVIL CODE SECTION 1542 IN THE JANUARY 14, 2019 SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT IS ONLY APPLICABLE TO THE EXTENT THERE IS A GENERAL RELEASE OF CLAIMS; IT DOES NOT CONSTITUTE A RELEASE ON ITS OWN. THE PLAIN LANGUAGE OF THE SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT IN EXHIBIT A ONLY RELEASES PLAINTIFF'S CLAIMS, BY WAY OF A DISMISSAL WITH PREJUDICE. IT DOES NOT PROVIDE FOR A GENERAL WAIVER OF ANY CLAIMS DEFENDANTS MAY HAVE AGAINST PLAINTIFF. FURTHERMORE, THERE IS NO OBJECTIVE MANIFESTATION OF AN AGREEMENT TO MUTUALLY RELEASE CLAIMS AS REQUIRED FOR SUCH A TERM TO BE PART OF AN ENFORCEABLE CONTRACT. (SEE BOWERS V. RAYMOND J. LUCIA COMPANIES, INC. (2012) 206 CAL.APP.4TH 724, 732-33 ["THE EXISTENCE OF MUTUAL CONSENT IS DETERMINED BY OBJECTIVE RATHER THAN SUBJECTIVE CRITERIA."].) A SETTLEMENT IS ONLY ENFORCEABLE WITH RESPECT TO MATERIAL TERMS AGREED TO BY ALL PARTIES. "THE COURT RULING ON THE MOTION [TO ENFORCE SETTLEMENT] MAY CONSIDER THE PARTIES' DECLARATIONS AND OTHER EVIDENCE IN DECIDING WHAT TERMS THE PARTIES AGREED TO. . . ." (HINES V. LUKES (2008) 167 CAL.APP.4TH 1174, 1182.) HERE, DEFENDANT BRIAN MCWILLIAMS DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS PLAINTIFF'S ASSERTION THAT THERE WAS AN AGREEMENT TO MUTUALLY RELEASE CLAIMS. (MCWILLIAMS DECL. 5.) THE ONLY BARGAINED-FOR EXCHANGE-AND THEREFORE THE ONLY AGREEMENT SUPPORTED BY CONSIDERATION-IS THE RELEASE OF PLAINTIFF'S CLAIMS RELATED TO THE CURRENT DISPUTE IN RETURN FOR $20,000. (NELSON DECL. EXH. A; CIV. CODE 1550.)(AS FULLY REPORTED IN THE REPORTER'S TRANSCRIPT) ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: M. Notice Of And Motion To Enforce Settlement Per Ccp 664.6
Off Calendar Apr-08-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Sheila Gropper Nelson, Esq. of Resolution Law Firm 415-362-2221 for Plaintiff.
Cory Birnberg, Esq. 415-398-1040 for Defendant Brian McWilliams.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views. Plaintiff argues breach. Defendant argues tender. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF ALLAN QUITON's Motion To Enforce Settlement Per Ccp 664.6.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. DENIED. The Settlement Agreement of January 14, 2019 does not contain any language binding the parties to enter into a mutual release of claims. The waiver of Civil Code section 1542 in the January 14, 2019 Settlement Agreement is only applicable to the extent there is a general release of claims; it does not constitute a release on its own. The plain language of the Settlement Agreement in Exhibit A only releases Plaintiff's claims, by way of a dismissal with prejudice. It does not provide for a general waiver of any claims Defendants may have against Plaintiff.

Furthermore, there is no objective manifestation of an agreement to mutually release claims as required for such a term to be part of an enforceable contract. (See Bowers v. Raymond J. Lucia Companies, Inc. (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th 724, 732-33 ["The existence of mutual consent is determined by objective rather than subjective criteria."].) A settlement is only enforceable with respect to material terms agreed to by all parties. "The court ruling on the motion [to enforce settlement] may consider the parties' declarations and other evidence in deciding what terms the parties agreed to. . . ." (Hines v. Lukes (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1182.) Here, Defendant Brian McWilliams directly contradicts Plaintiff's assertion that there was an agreement to mutually release claims. (McWilliams Decl. 5.) The only bargained-for exchange-and therefore the only agreement supported by consideration-is the release of Plaintiff's claims related to the current dispute in return for $20,000. (Nelson Decl. Exh. A; Civ. Code 1550.)(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

RULING: The tentative ruling is contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT BRIAN MCWILLIAMS' Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint is DENIED. All of the cross-claims that Mr. McWilliams seeks to file are barred by the absolute litigation privilege for statements made in the course of litigation. (Silberg v. Anderson (1990) 50 Cal. 3d 205). The only exception to this privilege is for a malicious prosecution claim, which Mr. McWilliams has stated that he is not seeking to file.

In addition, pursuant to Mr. Birnberg's request, the Court allowed Mr. Birnberg to submit a proposed order removing any cloud or slander on his client's property.

MINI MINUTES:

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT BRIAN MCWILLIAMS' MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT as follows: Defendant McWilliams' motion to strike the first amended complaint is granted. The court clearly stated that plaintiff only had leave to file the proposed amended complaint and plaintiff concedes that he filed a different pleading. (RT, 3:15-22; 4:15-16.) "A trial court has authority to strike sham pleadings, or those not filed in conformity with its prior ruling." (Janis v. Calif. State Lottery Com. (1996) 68 Cal.App.4th 824, 829.) Plaintiff has leave to file a second amended complaint that is identical to the proposed pleading by September 1, 2017. If Plaintiff seeks to file a different pleading, he must file and serve a motion to amend that complies with CRC 3.1324. Order signed. Judge: Suzanne R. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Amend Complaint
Off Calendar Jul-05-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Sheila Gropper Nelson, Esq. (415-362-2221) of Revolution Law Firm for Plaintiff.
Cory Birnberg, Esq. (415-398-1040) of Birnberg & Associates for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court informs parties of two basic issues. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF ALLAN QUITON's Motion To Amend Complaint. Plaintiff Allan Quiton's motion to amend complaint is granted. California's liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings and the absence of any discernable prejudice to defendant Brian McWilliams strongly favors granting the motion. Mr. McWilliams' arguments regarding the sufficiency of the new allegations are best addressed on a pleading motion such as a demurrer or motion to strike or perhaps a motion for summary judgment/adjudication. Mr. Quiton is permitted to revive his fraud claim and to re-allege liability against Doe defendants because he dismissed those allegations when he sought a default judgment. It would be unfair and contrary to California policy to preclude assertion of those allegations now that the default judgment has been vacated. The addition of the corporate defendant and alter ego allegations are permitted because California law allows such allegations even after entry of judgment. Order is signed.
